content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 9, 2010

Ms. Lisa Stephens, Treasurer
Alliance for a Green and Livable Downtown
2150 Channing Way #21
Berkeley, CA 94704

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-002

Dear Ms. Stephens:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please be aware that the
Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may
have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Are the Alliance for Green and Livable Downtown's efforts raising and spending
money to gather signatures to prevent the implementation of a new Downtown Plan by
the Berkeley City Council reportable under the Act, where the Council has not taken any
action to place a measure concerning the Downtown Plan on the ballot?

CONCLUSION

The Downtown Plan issue has not become a "measure" under the Act, because the
Council has not taken action to place the measure on the ballot. Although signatures
were gathered to prevent the Downtown Plan ordinance from taking effect under
Berkeley's referendum process, efforts to block the ordinance do not meet the Act's other
definition of measure which is a "proposition which is submitted or is intended to be
submitted to a popular vote at an election by initiative, referendum or recall procedure."

___________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-002
Page No. 2


The Downtown Plan referendum becomes a "measure" under the Act only if the
Berkeley City Council takes action to place it on the ballot. If the Berkeley City Council
places the Downtown Plan on the ballot, the Alliance for Green and Livable Downtown
would have reporting obligations, as discussed below, if it has received or spent $1,000
or more to support or oppose the measure during a calendar year.

FACTS

The Alliance for Green and Livable Downtown is an organization that was
established to raise and spend money to gather signatures to stop the enacting of a new
Downtown Plan by the Berkeley City Council. Under the referendum clause in the
Berkeley City Charter, any ordinance or other legislative action taken by the Berkeley
City Council can be halted if the requisite number of signatures is gathered within 30
days of the action's effective date. As with other petitions, the Alameda County
Registrar of Voters counts and certifies that the threshold number of signatures has been
met. Unlike initiatives, recalls, or other referenda in various cities and counties, a
successful referendum signature drive does not place a measure directly on the Berkeley
ballots. Instead, it must go back to the Berkeley City Council for disposition. The
Council has two options: to place the action on the ballot of the next regular election or
to rescind it.

When you began the signature gathering process, you were acting under the belief
that the referendum signature drive did not qualify as a "measure" under the Act. As
such, you believe that you are not required to file as a committee or report your financial
activities. You base your conclusion on past and current advice from this agency's
telephone assistance line. After the referendum process began, your Deputy City Clerk
expressed a different opinion. You are requesting written advice to clarify the matter.

You believe that the referendum becomes a measure only if the Berkeley City
Council takes action to place it on the ballot. "Referendum" is used liberally to refer to a
number of different processes in California. In your situation, you are challenging a
legislative action to prevent it from taking effect. A successful signature gathering effort
can directly place the Downtown Plan on the ballot, and the City Council is not
required to place it on the ballot, either – they can rescind their previous action, and
nothing goes on the ballot as a result. As such, you believe the referendum becomes a
"measure" only if the Berkeley City Council takes action to place it on the ballot, and that
your actions to date are not subject to the filing requirements of a campaign committee.
Up to this point, your efforts have been to influence the decision making process of the
Berkeley City Council. The Alliance for a Green and Livable Downtown did not intend
to become a "campaign committee."

In late August, the Alameda County Registrar of Voters certified that you had the
required number of signatures to stop the implementation of the City Council approved

File No. A-10-002
Page No. 3


Downtown Plan under the referendum clause of the Berkeley City Charter. You expected
the City Council to take one of the two actions open to them, either to place Downtown
Plan on the ballot or to rescind it. As of this date, they have done neither, instead
choosing to take no action.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires candidates and committees to file periodic reports disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made in connection with state and local
elections. "Committee" includes any person or group of persons that receives
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of supporting or
opposing candidates or for the qualification, passage, or defeat of a ballot measure.
(Sections 82013(a) and 82015; Regulation 18215.)

The term "measure" is defined in Section 82043 as follows:

"'Measure' means any constitutional amendment or other proposition
which is submitted to a popular vote at an election by action of a
legislative body, or which is submitted or is intended to be submitted to a
popular vote at an election by initiative, referendum or recall procedures
whether or not it qualifies for the ballot."

In its Fontana Opinion, (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 25, the Commission held that a
proposal submitted to the voters by a legislative body does not become a "measure"
under the Act until the legislative body orders it placed on the ballot. The Commission
further held that persons raising or spending funds to support or oppose the measure have
no campaign disclosure obligations until that time.² A constitutional amendment or other
proposition which is submitted or intended to be submitted to a popular vote at an
election by initiative, referendum, or recall procedure becomes a "measure" under the Act
when the proponents begin to circulate signature petitions to qualify the measure for the
ballot.

Once a proposal becomes a measure, all contributions received and expenditures
made must be reported, including those received and made before the campaign reporting
requirements were triggered. (Angus Advice Letter, No. A-97-173.) The terms
"contribution" and "expenditure" are defined as payments made for "political purposes."
(Sections 82015 and 82025.) "Political purpose" means that the payment was made for
the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure. (Regulations 18215(a)(1) and 18225(a)(1).)

² Note that the conclusion in the Fontana Opinion concerning when reporting is required of
contributions and expenditures to support or oppose a Local Area Formation Commission (LAFCO)
proposal has been changed by legislation specifically addressing LAFCO reporting. (Sections 84250-
84252.)

File No. A-10-002
Page No. 4


The first filing required for a group collecting funds for political purposes is the
committee statement of organization (Form 410), due within 10 days of receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year. (Section 84101.) Reading this
provision in conjunction with the Fontana Opinion, however, the 10-day filing period for
a group that raises $1,000 in contributions prior to an issue becoming a measure would
not begin to run until the issue is placed on the ballot or, in the case of a proposition
which is submitted or is intended to be submitted to a popular vote at an election by
initiative, referendum or recall procedure, until the proponents begin to circulate
signature petitions. (Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-00-075.) If the issue never becomes
a measure, no filing obligations will be incurred.

The facts of the Bisek Advice Letter, No. A-93-185, are similar to those of the
question at hand. In Bisek, the South Pasadena Taxpayers Association ("SPTA")
opposed the formation of benefit assessment district and a fire suppression district.
SPTA asked if it would incur campaign reporting requirements if it raised money to pay
for mailers that would include a "protest form" to defeat a proposed benefit assessment
district. Under the relevant property owner protest rules if a certain number of property
owners sign protest petitions, the City Council must decide either to place the issue on an
election ballot or to terminate the tax proposal. We advised that money raised or spent by
SPTA to gather petition signatures to protest the district assessments are not reportable
until one of the issues is placed on an election ballot.

Your situation involves the referendum clause of the Berkeley City Charter.
Article XIV, Section 93 of the Berkeley City Charter entitled the "mode of protesting
against ordinances" states:

"No ordinance passed by the Council shall go into effect before thirty
days from the time of its final passage except when otherwise required by
the general laws of the State or by the provisions of this Charter respecting
street improvements, and except the ordinance making the annual tax levy,
and except an ordinance for the immediate preservation of the public
peace, health or safety, which contains a statement of its urgency, and is
passed by a seven-ninths (7/9) vote of the Council, provided that no grant
of any franchise shall be construed to be an urgency measure, but all
franchises shall be subject to the referendum vote herein provided. If
during said thirty days a petition signed by qualified electors of the City
equal in number to at least ten percent of the entire vote cast for all
candidates for Mayor at the last preceding general municipal election at
which a Mayor was elected, protesting against the passage of such
ordinance, be presented to the Council, the same shall thereupon be
suspended from going into operation and it shall be the duty of the
Council to reconsider such ordinance, and if the same be not entirely
repealed, the council shall submit the ordinance, as is provided in Article

File No. A-10-002
Page No. 5

XIII of the Charter, to the vote of the electors of the city, at the next
occurring regular statewide or general or special municipal election
providing no posting, publication or other legal deadline as set forth in the
California Elections Code and Government Code, has expired at the time
the City Clerk reports verification of the petition to the City Council, and
such ordinance shall not go into effect or become operative unless a
majority of the qualified electors voting on the same shall vote in favor
thereof. . . ."

Under the referendum clause in the Charter, any ordinance or other legislative
action taken by the Berkeley City Council can be halted if the requisite number of
signatures is gathered within 30 days of the action's effective date. This referendum
process may not automatically result in a measure being placed on the local ballot.
Under the City Charter provision, if sufficient protest petition signatures are received, the
Council may reconsider the ordinance, repeal it, or place it on the ballot for a vote. The
Downtown Plan issue has not become a "measure" under the Act, because the Berkeley
City Council has not acted to place it on the ballot. The Downtown Plan ordinance did
not become a "measure" under the Act when signature gathering to halt the enactment of
the ordinance started because the ordinance at this point is not a "proposition which is
submitted or is intended to be submitted to a popular vote at an election by initiative,
referendum or recall procedure." Although signatures are gathered for Berkeley's
referendum process, the end result prevents a law from taking effect, it does not place a
measure on the local ballot.

The Alameda County Registrar of Voters certified that your organization had the
required number of signatures to stop the implementation of the City Council approved
Downtown Plan under the referendum clause of the Berkeley City Charter. As of this
date, the City Council has not chosen to place the Downtown Plan on the ballot or to
rescind it. Instead the Council has taken no action on the matter.

We agree that the Downtown Plan referendum becomes a "measure" under the
Act only if the Berkeley City Council takes action to place it on the ballot. If the
Berkeley City Council places the Downtown Plan on the ballot, the Alliance for Green
and Livable Downtowns would be required to file a Form 410 statement of organization as
a political committee, and report all contributions received and expenditures made for the
purpose of influencing the voters for or against the measure if the Alliance has received
or spent $1,000 or more for this purpose in a calendar year.

Although the obligation to report contributions received or expenditures made in
support of or in opposition to a ballot measure does not begin until the matter becomes a
"measure," we have advised that contributions received and expenditures made prior to
that time may need to be recaptured on the first campaign report. Contributions and
expenditures that predate the filing of the first campaign statement are reportable if they

File No. A-10-002
Page No. 6

were intended to influence the voters to vote for or against the measure. (Martini Advice
Letter, No. A-93-378; Hicks Advice Letter, No. I-98-007.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division

HPW:jgl
